_meta.title: Quiver Capability Explorer
_meta.subtitle: One tab per Quiver track — each routed to the best model we found, with its empirical operating envelope on every result. 9 tracks.
_meta.banner: These are commodity tools that enrich Quiver's insights — they do not replace the V1-T + functional-trace moat. 'State-of-the-art on shit is still shit': every verdict below is empirical on real Quiver substrate, not paper benchmarks.

reliable.label: Reliable
reliable.desc: Use the number; verdict is well-supported on Quiver substrate.

caution.label: Use with caution
caution.desc: Best available, but marginal or directional — read the envelope.

split.label: Split verdict
split.desc: Trust one direction only (e.g. yes vs no, or ranking vs prediction).

low_value.label: Low value
low_value.desc: Works but not a Quiver bottleneck — deprioritized.

build.label: Build, don't buy
build.desc: Nothing public works (and nothing public can). This is the moat.

dont_use.label: Do not use
dont_use.desc: Worse than chance / wrong task — do not ship.

tracks.label: Protein Family Clustering
tracks.question: Given a panel of genes, which embeddings put same-family genes together?
tracks.tagline: Protein sequence(s) → embedding + nearest reference family
tracks.best_model_detail: ESM-2-650M (best-layer ~idx 8/33, mean-centered). MAMMAL 458M is at parity; SaProt / ProstT5 are GPCR specialists.

verdict.headline: Reliable for family grouping — but layer selection is the real lever, not model choice.
verdict.why: On the 40-gene panel ESM-2-650M (0.875) ≈ MAMMAL 458M (0.850) ≈ ESM-2 3B/15B (0.850): a 4-way near-tie at saturation. A 23× parameter increase moves nothing. The naive last-layer mean-pool undersells every encoder by ~0.10—extract an early-mid layer (~20-25% depth) and mean-center.
verdict.recommended_use: Use ESM-2-650M (smallest, MIT) or MAMMAL, layer-selected + centered. Use SaProt/ProstT5 for GPCR-family hits (perfect 8/8). Function-defined families (E3 ligases, nuclear receptors) are a universal ceiling for sequence-only models at any size. TESTED 2026-06-15: ProTrek-650M (MIT, function-aware trimodal PLM) is NOT a clustering upgrade (0.725 < ESM-2-650M 0.875), but its TEXT-anchored family assignment cracks the function-defined families that defeat sequence-only models — E3-ligase 0.75, nuclear-receptor 1.0. Use ProTrek text-assignment (not sequence clustering) for E3/NR.

inputs.name: sequences
inputs.label: Protein sequence(s) — one per line, FASTA-style (optional ',name')

tracks.example_note: lysozyme + calmodulin — distinct families should land on distinct reference clusters.

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire ESM-2-650M embedding extraction on AWS to populate this.

tracks.label: DTI / Binder Triage
tracks.question: Given a Quiver target and a compound, does it bind?
tracks.tagline: Target (UniProt or sequence) + compound SMILES → binding score (BALM triage → Boltz-2 confirm)
tracks.best_model: Per-target CNS binder fine-tunes (LIVE) → Boltz-2 confirm
tracks.best_model_detail: Per-target Morgan-FP+GradientBoosting binder fine-tunes — one per data-rich CNS target (Nav/Cav/NMDA + mTOR-pathway + GPCR + kinase), trained on ChEMBL+GtoPdb, served LIVE in this app (CPU, no AWS). Family-mean scaffold-split CV AUROC 0.95–0.99 vs zero-shot 0.50 on ion channels. BALM/PLAPT (zero-shot) are the fallback for targets without a fine-tune; Boltz-2 (Track 3) confirms structure/selectivity and novel out-of-domain chemotypes.

performance.headline: 18 LIVE per-target binder fine-tunes (16 ChEMBL-curated 0.95–0.997 + 2 PubChem-qHTS rescues 0.79–0.81) vs zero-shot 0.50

verdict.headline: Per-target binder fine-tunes are LIVE and strong in-domain (0.95–0.99 scaffold-split) — the deployable answer where data exists; gate by Tanimoto-to-train and hand novel chemotypes to Boltz-2.
verdict.why: The campaign's lever, deployed: per-target FP+GBT fine-tunes hit family-mean scaffold-split CV AUROC 0.95–0.99 (Nav 0.993, mTOR 0.993, GPCR 0.995, kinase 0.992, NMDA 0.979, Cav 0.953) — vs off-the-shelf zero-shot 0.50 on ion channels. They run live in this app (CPU joblibs, no GPU/AWS). HONEST LIMIT: like all ligand-QSAR they collapse off-distribution — e.g. suzetrigine (a novel 2024 Nav1.8 scaffold absent from ChEMBL, Tanimoto 0.41 to nearest active) is under-recognized (P≈0.14). So every call carries a Tanimoto-to-train confidence flag; low/medium-confidence (novel) compounds are a weak prior to confirm structurally with Boltz-2. VALIDATION CAVEAT: scaffold-split CV on PUBLIC data (ChEMBL/PubChem/TDC) — strong generalization to novel public scaffolds, but NOT yet validated on Quiver's own measured binders. The one test against real Quiver experimental hits (TSC2 deconvolution) failed out-of-domain — so treat low/medium-confidence (novel-chemotype) calls as weak priors until a Quiver held-out panel is scored.
verdict.recommended_use: Use the per-target fine-tune's P(binder) for data-rich CNS targets (Nav1.7/1.8/1.2/1.5, NMDA, mTOR-pathway incl. PKM2/PPARD, DRD2/HTR2A, GSK3B/LRRK2/BACE1) — high-confidence (in-domain) calls are reliable; treat low/medium-confidence (novel-scaffold) calls as weak priors and confirm with Boltz-2 (Track 3). For data-POOR channels (Nav1.1/Dravet, Nav1.6, Kv7.2/epilepsy) no public fine-tune is possible — generate Quiver screening data (see docs/cns_finetune_readiness.md). Off-the-shelf BALM/PLAPT zero-shot remains a fallback for un-fine-tuned targets (kinase/mTOR ~0.72–0.80, ion channels 0.50).

inputs.name: target_seq
inputs.label: …or paste a target protein sequence instead

tracks.example_note: suzetrigine → Nav1.8 (SCN10A) — the named test off-the-shelf DTI heads fail and Boltz-2 partially recovers.

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire BALM (cosine) on AWS; PLAPT for a fast first pass; Boltz-2 (Track 3) to confirm.

tracks.label: Structure-Based Binding
tracks.question: Does it bind — co-folded, so it works on novel pockets BindingDB doesn't cover?
tracks.tagline: Target sequence + ligand SMILES → co-folded complex + affinity
tracks.best_model_detail: Boltz-2 (structure + affinity in one model). Folded into Track 2; no separate model needed.

verdict.headline: Boltz-2 co-folding — the structure-based route for pockets BindingDB can't reach.
verdict.why: Boltz-2 predicts the complex AND an affinity in one pass, so it works where no binding-data baseline can (novel pockets, no training pairs). Literature rates it as approaching FEP accuracy and beating AF3/Chai-1 on affinity. Same empirical envelope as Track 2.
verdict.recommended_use: Use for structure-based triage on targets without binding data. NOTE: deep co-folding campaigns (TSC2, DFP compounds) live on the boltz branch — this tab routes to the same Boltz-2 AWS endpoint.

tracks.example_note: suzetrigine + a Nav1.8 pocket fragment — co-fold demo.

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire Boltz-2 co-folding on an AWS GPU (A100/L4) to populate complex + affinity.

tracks.label: BBB Penetrance
tracks.question: Will a CNS compound actually cross the blood-brain barrier?
tracks.tagline: SMILES → P(BBB+); MapLight primary, MolFormer-XL backstop
tracks.best_model_detail: MapLight (CatBoost on ECFP+Avalon+ErG+RDKit descriptors) — confirmed held-out on B3DB. MolFormer-XL is the secondary cross-check; MAMMAL the 'trust the no' specificity backstop.

performance.headline: MapLight-class model, LIVE: scaffold-test AUROC 0.903 (≈ MapLight 0.91)

verdict.headline: MapLight is the BBBP primary — confirmed on a held-out dataset, best calibration + best OOD-robustness. Ship a Tanimoto-to-train confidence flag.
verdict.why: MapLight (fingerprint+CatBoost) beats MolFormer-XL on TDC scaffold (0.905 vs 0.889) AND on an INDEPENDENT held-out set (B3DB 0.919 vs 0.854, 6,142 leakage-removed cpds), with the best calibration (Brier 0.106) and far-OOD AUROC (0.674 vs MolFormer's near-chance 0.590). Fingerprint+GBT beats the SMILES LM on this substructure-driven endpoint. CPU-only, commercial-OK. VALIDATION: TDC scaffold-split (public) — not yet validated on Quiver compounds; gate by the Tanimoto-to-train confidence flag.
verdict.recommended_use: LIVE in-app: a MapLight-class FP+GBT model (Morgan+MACCS+RDKit-desc) gives a real BBB probability (scaffold-test AUROC 0.903 ≈ MapLight 0.91), Tanimoto-to-train confidence-gated. Default to MapLight's probability; attach a Tanimoto-to-train confidence flag (near-domain AUROC ~0.96, far-OOD ~0.67 — flag low-confidence novel scaffolds). Keep MolFormer-XL as a secondary cross-check and MAMMAL's confident 'no' as a specificity backstop.

batch.label: Drug SMILES — one per line (CSV ok: first column SMILES, optional 2nd = name)

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire MapLight (primary) + MolFormer-XL backstop on AWS. Attach Tanimoto-to-train confidence flag.

tracks.label: Toxicity / DILI / hERG
tracks.question: Is this compound likely to fail Phase 1 on safety?
tracks.tagline: SMILES → hERG + DILI risk (ClinTox dropped — it's worse than chance)
tracks.best_model_detail: ChemBERTa-2 (hERG+DILI, one commercial model) + ADMET-AI (DILI gate) + MapLight (most OOD-robust hERG, far-OOD 0.809). ClinTox dropped (worse than chance across 4 models).

verdict.headline: Reliable de-risking gate — use hERG + DILI, never ClinTox.
verdict.why: MapLight (FP+CatBoost) is the most OOD-robust hERG model we've measured (far-OOD AUROC 0.809 vs the LMs' 0.56–0.61) — it degrades least on novel chemotypes, the failure mode that sinks a de-risking gate. ChemBERTa-2 gives one commercial-OK model for hERG (0.726) + DILI (ext TPR 0.73); ADMET-AI adds a strong DILI gate (TPR 0.83). ClinTox (failed-trial label) does NOT transfer to real withdrawals (external AUROC 0.24–0.47 across MAMMAL/MolFormer/ChemBERTa/TxGemma). TxGemma-9B is the ~0.95 ceiling but research-only. VALIDATION: TDC scaffold-split (public) — not yet validated on Quiver compounds; gate by the Tanimoto-to-train confidence flag.
verdict.recommended_use: LIVE in-app: MapLight-class FP+GBT hERG (0.881) + DILI (0.934) scaffold-test, Tanimoto-gated. Run hERG (MapLight for OOD robustness; ChemBERTa-2 in-domain) + DILI (ADMET-AI / ChemBERTa-2) as a Phase-1 safety gate; treat a high score as a flag, not a kill. Drop ClinTox. CardioGenAI's NaV1.5/CaV1.2 multi-channel concept is appealing but its heads are uncalibrated + vocab-locked (not adopted).

batch.label: Drug SMILES — one per line (CSV ok: first column SMILES, optional 2nd = name)

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire ChemBERTa-2 + ADMET-AI on AWS. ClinTox intentionally omitted (dead).

tracks.label: KG / Hypothesis Generation
tracks.question: What is connected to this CRISPR-N hit in the literature/KG?
tracks.tagline: Target gene (+ optional known drug) → re-ranked shortlist of KG-connected drugs
tracks.best_model_detail: PROTON (NeuroKG bilinear decoder, known-drug ranking) + ULTRA (zero-shot KG FM that fixes PROTON's hub bias AND adds inductive novel-target capability). Ranking direction only.

performance.headline: PROTON ranks known drugs (median 4.3%); ULTRA fixes hub bias + does novel targets

verdict.headline: Co-winners on the SAME graph: PROTON ranks known drugs; ULTRA (zero-shot) fixes PROTON's hub bias and adds novel-target capability. Ranking-shortlist tools, not predictors.
verdict.why: On NeuroKG (PROTON's own training graph), ULTRA — a zero-shot KG foundation model — fixes both of PROTON's documented failures: no hub bias (per-target top-10 Jaccard 0.065; its top drugs are correct pharmacology — kinase inhibitors for kinases, etc. — not Bepridil-for-everything) and real inductive novel-target capability (median 0.34% with a target's edges held out, where PROTON has zero capability). PROTON still ranks KNOWN drugs well (median 4.3%) on well-studied targets. Both remain ranking-shortlist tools — PROTON's forward 'top drug' prediction is hub noise and is never surfaced.
verdict.recommended_use: Use ULTRA as the default for novel/weakly-connected targets, hub-sensitive shortlists, and ion-channel targets; PROTON for known-drug ranking against well-studied NeuroKG targets. Never expose forward 'top drug' predictions. Neither is trustworthy on kinases. EMET (BenchSci) is the same use case at $150-500K/yr (wrong-fit).

inputs.name: known_drug
inputs.label: Known drug to rank (optional — ranking direction)

tracks.example_note: DRD2 / haloperidol — a well-studied target where the ranking direction works (median 2.6%).

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire PROTON ranking on AWS. Ranking direction only; forward 'top drug' prediction is intentionally NOT surfaced (hub noise).

tracks.label: Generative Chemistry
tracks.question: Hit-to-lead expansion / analog generation.
tracks.tagline: Seed SMILES → nearest-neighbour analogs (Morgan FP + Enamine REAL)
tracks.best_model_detail: Morgan fingerprints + Enamine REAL nearest-neighbour. Boring, works; beats MAMMAL embeddings.

verdict.headline: Morgan FP + Enamine REAL is the boring winner — but generation isn't a Quiver bottleneck.
verdict.why: MAMMAL public weights are span-infillers, not de-novo generators (1/8 exact recovery). Morgan-fingerprint similarity beats MAMMAL embedding similarity 0.96 vs 0.72 for analog expansion. Quiver's bottleneck is target-ID and triage, not novel-structure generation.
verdict.recommended_use: Use Morgan FP nearest-neighbour over Enamine REAL for hit-to-lead expansion. Deprioritized — revisit only after Tracks 4-5 ship to the Explorer.

tracks.example_note: aspirin — expand to near analogs by Morgan similarity.

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire Morgan FP nearest-neighbour over Enamine REAL to populate analogs.

tracks.label: Off-Target / Selectivity
tracks.question: Does this compound only hit Nav1.8, or also Nav1.5/1.7?
tracks.tagline: Compound SMILES + target panel → per-paralog selectivity ranking
tracks.best_model_detail: Boltz-2 across a paralog panel (folded into Track 2). Ranks Nav1.8 #1 but with narrow margins.

verdict.headline: Boltz-2 gets the right paralog order — but margins are too narrow to call selectivity.
verdict.why: Boltz-2 ranks suzetrigine's Nav1.8 #1 across 9 SCN paralogs, but margins are narrow (0.31–0.44) — directionally right, quantitatively soft. SEQUENCE DTI CANNOT do fine selectivity: BALM biases to Nav1.5, and on Ben's PKM2-vs-PPARD target deconvolution both BALM and PLAPT are at chance (controls outscore binders). Fine selectivity/deconvolution needs co-folding (Boltz-2) or Quiver's own functional/DFP signatures.
verdict.recommended_use: Use Boltz-2 to rank a compound across a paralog panel for DIRECTIONAL selectivity (a hypothesis, not a ratio). Do NOT use sequence DTI (BALM/PLAPT) for selectivity or target deconvolution — it's at chance. Quiver's functional/DFP signatures are the deconvolution route.

inputs.name: targets
inputs.label: Target panel — UniProt accessions, one per line (CSV: acc, name)
inputs.placeholder:
Q9Y5Y9, Nav1.8 (SCN10A)
Q15858, Nav1.7 (SCN9A)
Q14524, Nav1.5 (SCN5A)

example.targets:
Q9Y5Y9, Nav1.8 (SCN10A)
Q15858, Nav1.7 (SCN9A)
Q14524, Nav1.5 (SCN5A)

tracks.example_note: suzetrigine across Nav1.8 / 1.7 / 1.5 — Nav1.8 should rank first.

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire Boltz-2 paralog-panel scoring on an AWS GPU to populate this.

tracks.label: Variant Effect (Channelopathy)
tracks.question: Is this ion-channel missense variant gain- or loss-of-function?
tracks.tagline: Gene + missense variant → GoF / LoF call (funNCion; Nav1.8 = build-don't-buy)
tracks.best_model_detail: funNCion (Apache-2.0, ion-channel GoF/LoF classifier, AUROC 0.897). MissION (0.925) is portal-only (not adoptable). A generic pLM (ESM-2 LLR) is insufficient (0.665). Nav1.8/SCN10A is absent from public sets → Quiver V1-T fine-tune is the moat.

performance.headline: funNCion AUROC 0.897 · generic pLM (ESM-2 LLR) only 0.665

verdict.headline: funNCion is the adoptable ion-channel GoF/LoF model for public channels. A generic pLM can't do it. Nav1.8 isn't in any public set — that's the moat.
verdict.why: Target deconvolution of variant FUNCTION: funNCion (Apache-2.0) calls GoF vs LoF on ion-channel missense at AUROC 0.897 and is downloadable. MissION (0.925) is marginally better but web-portal-only (no repo/weights) → not adoptable. A generic protein LM (ESM-2 650M masked-marginal LLR) scores only 0.665 — it captures deleteriousness, NOT GoF-vs-LoF direction (best Quiver channel SCN5A 0.737). Critically, SCN10A (Nav1.8) + SCN3A are ABSENT from every public training set, so no public model calls GoF/LoF on Quiver's flagship channel. EMPIRICAL 2026-06-15: a supervised GoF/LoF fine-tune on pooled SCION+funNCion Nav/Cav variants (2,212) does NOT generalize across genes (leave-one-gene-out 0.36 < ESM-2-LLR baseline 0.61) and does NOT transfer to held-out Nav1.8 (0.48 ≈ chance). Direction is channel-specific — Nav1.8 GoF/LoF needs Quiver functional data (build-don't-buy, now demonstrated not just asserted).
verdict.recommended_use: Use funNCion for GoF/LoF on the public channels (SCN1A/2A/5A, etc.); do NOT rely on a generic pLM. For Nav1.8 — build, don't buy: a variant-effect model fine-tuned on Quiver's own V1-T functional readouts is the moat (same thesis as the Nav binder fine-tune). MissION is a demo, not a pipeline component.

tracks.example_note: SCN2A R1882Q — a known gain-of-function epilepsy variant (funNCion calls direction; ESM-2 LLR can't).

stub_prediction.note: STUB — wire funNCion on AWS. Generic pLM (ESM-2 LLR) intentionally NOT the primary (0.665, direction-blind). Nav1.8 unsupported by public models.
